segments.text: Yeah, you just want to
Right.
The meeting is resumed.
That's 1,000 if it ends.
So we're
hearing evidence in the Fisheries Amendment Bill.
We've got 12.
First up, we have
Steve Abel of the Green Party of Aotearoa New Zealand.
You've got-
Five
10 minutes.
How you use that time is up to you.
Suggest you leave some time for questions at the end.
Hard questions.
So away you go.
Tēnā koutou katoa. Thank you very much for the opportunity to present today.
I'm speaking on behalf of Green co-leader Chlöe
Swarbrick, who could not be with us this morning.
The Green Party are here today to emphasize to the committee
the public outrage at this Fisheries Amendment Bill.
While New Zealanders are demanding relief from a cost of living crisis,
this government has chosen to introduce a bill that fundamentally
alters the purpose and direction of fisheries management in
Aotearoa.
At its heart, this bill is about who this government works for.
And on that question, the answer is very clear.
This is a government that has not yet met a part of the environment
it won't try to open up, extract from, and convert
into short-term profit,
regardless of the long-term consequences and regardless of who pays the
price. Because the reality is the price is not
paid by industry. The price is paid by everyday New
Zealanders and by the environment.
It is paid by people who just want to go fishing with their whanau.
It is paid by our communities who rely on healthy
ecosystems, and it is paid by future generations who inherit what
we leave behind.
Here in Aotearoa, we pride ourselves on a simple idea that you
can go out and catch a feed for your family.
That access to a healthy, thriving ocean is part of what
makes this place special.
This bill undermines that very birthright.
It treats fisheries not as something we all share, but as something to be
maximized, traded, and exported.
And that brings me to a second point.
This is about public resources. Our oceans and the species
that live therein are being handed over to
private interests.
But this bill continues a pattern from this government that is shifting
control away from the public and towards a small number of
well-connected commercial players.
It entrenches the power of quota holders.
It reduces the obligation to provide for the public before
allocating to industry. And it pushes everyday New Zealanders
closer to the margins of a system that they should be at the
center of.
This is not an accident. This is a political choice.
And it raises a simple question: Who is the system being
redesigned for?
Because it is not being redesigned for the public.
Overwhelmingly, public opposition has made that clear.
We already know that more than 27,000 people submitted on earlier
proposals, with over 90% opposition.
Nine out of 10 submitters said no.
And yet here we are. This bill is being brought forward anyway,
rushed with only some parts walked back after public
backlash. That is not good process.
That is not democratic accountability.
That is a government deciding it knows better than the people it
represents.
We've already seen one example of this, the proposal to remove minimum
size limits for commercial fishers.
Only after immense public pressure did the minister back down,
and we're yet to see that manifest in the actual legislation.
But New Zealanders need to understand that this was just one part
of a terrible bill. Because the rest of the bill still weakens
oversight, reduces transparency, and tilts the playing field towards
industry. This is about weakening accountability while protecting
industry. We know there are serious issues in the fishing sector.
When cameras were introduced onboard vessels, they revealed what had long
been suspected, underreporting
bycatch and waste is at much higher levels than
previously thought. That transparency was a step forward.
But now, instead of building on that progress, this bill goes
backwards. It limits public access to that footage, it restricts
scrutiny, and it introduces fines of up to
$50,000 for sharing that information.
Now we need to think about that.
They are proposing harsher penalties for exposing
potential wrongdoing than many of the penalties faced for the
wrongdoing itself.
That is a political signal about whose interests are being protected,
and it is not the public interest. Because the direction of
travel has been clear from the beginning.
Corporate interests over long-term sustainability.
This bill poses a question about the kind of country we
want to be.
Do we want to be a country where our ocean and nature
itself are treated as short-term
commodities? Or do we want to be a country that protects and
values the natural world so that everyone can share in it
now and in the future?
Do we want a system designed around export growth at
any cost?
This bill is a clear statement from this government that industry interests
come first. Public access comes second.
Environmental protections are negotiable.
And overwhelming public opposition can be
ignored.Green Party
fervently opposes this legislation.
We urge the committee to reject this bill, but more than
that, we'd only want to defend the status quo.
We can and should go further. We need to ban
bottom trawling and protect at least 30% of our oceans,
because there is no separating the economy from the environment
that sustains all of us.
Because New Zealanders deserve better than a system that sells off our environment,
weakens accountability, and sidelines the public voice.
We can have fisheries that are sustainable, transparent,
and fair, but this bill is not the way to get there.
Thank you.
Thank you, Steve.
Any questions?
Doesn't seem so.
Thank you very much.
Thank you very much.
We'll move on. Is the Parliamentary Commissioner
team here? Oh, excellent.
So next up, we have the Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment.
So we've got,
I assume it's Rebecca here in person, and you have
some online as well. Is that correct?
Mr. Chair, they should be here in person.
Okay.
I had the benefit of just being next door, so I was able to just walk straight
through. But I'm sure they'll be here in due course.
Okay.
Wouldn't want to hold the committee business up.
So
you have 20 minutes.
Kia ora. Thank you.
So, fire away.
Look, members, Mr. Chair, the commissioner is currently on
leave and cannot attend committee today. I'm sorry.
However, this oral submission has been signed off from him.
I'm Rebecca Thornley, the commissioner's general counsel, and
hopefully will be joined by several colleagues shortly.
This submission is made in the context of the PCE's functions
under Section 16 of the Environment Act,
undertaken with a view to maintain or improve the
environment, spanning the entire environmental management
system of which fisheries management is a part.
The PCE submission today will address three aspects that were not
covered in the written submission.
These are the impact on fisheries decisions, including
standard factors,
application of the Official Information Act, and access to
onboard camera footage,
and the interface between the Fisheries Act and the Resource Management
Act, which at the moment may be replaced by the Planning
Bill and Natural Environment Bill.
Turning to the first point of decision making.
This bill substantially changes the way that the minister will
make total allowable catch
decisions. While some of these changes are
discussed in the written submission,
I address the introduction of standard factors as
decision-making criteria.
Standard factors limit the extent to which the minister
can take into account environmental principles when
setting the TAC,
which otherwise apply to all persons exercising
functions and powers under the Act.
This was part of the fundamental design of the
These principles and the philosophy that
sustainability underpins TAC decisions are
consistent with New Zealand's international obligations under
UNCLOS, as originally endorsed by this very
committee in its final report on the Fisheries Bill in
Standard factors include the environmental conditions that affect
specified stock and the social,
cultural, and economic factors the minister considers
relevant.
By limiting the application of the environmental principles,
not prioritizing environmental factors over this list of
other matters,
and the reference to TAC being removed from sustainability
measures,
TAC decisions will fail to uphold the fundamental
purpose of fisheries management.
I'd like to introduce my colleague, Geoff Simmons, our Chief Advisor, Strategy and
Policy.
Kia ora. Just in time.
Over the long term, setting TAC should be entirely a matter of
responding to biophysical realities.
Imposing concepts like quota management areas and maximum sustainable
yield for single stocks onto complex and
mobile biological systems isn't
straightforward. As EDS points out, in the
absence of other tools addressing localized biophysical
issues will sometimes require maintaining or building a
greater abundance of stock than maximum sustainable yield
would suggest.
Social, cultural, and economic factors don't alter that
biophysical reality. If adjustments in TAC are
needed, it may be possible to do that in a way that considers
social, cultural, and economic realities in the short
term.
However, in the long term, consideration of social, cultural,
and economic factors will detract from the necessary focus on
biophysical realities to the detriment of both the marine
environment and fisheries.
We acknowledge that the TAC may not always be the ideal
place to embed the consideration of all environmental impacts
of fishing.Ideally, there would be greater use of
spatial planning,
particularly different kinds of marine reserves, and potentially
some maximum size limits for catch in certain areas.
Managing fisheries solely through TAC decisions is really
our second-best policy response.
However, in lieu of other spatial protection tools, the
TAC is all that we have.
Mm.
And our recommendations on this issue are to not exclude
TAC from sustainability measures,
do not limit the consideration of environmental principles to the
standard factors, and make explicit that environmental
conditions affecting stock is the primary consideration, but
the minister may have regard to social, cultural, and economic
factors.
Mr. Chair, moving to the application of the OIA and access to
onboard camera footage.
The bill proposes to exclude application of the OIA to
camera recordings and instead provides limited
circumstances in which disclosure may occur.
The policy rationale does not establish the risk that this
amendment seeks to address based on the number of
OIA requests that have been made and the volume of camera
footage that has been disclosed since the requirement was
introduced. As we understand it, as at
12 March 2025, MPI had received
14 OIA requests, but we are not aware of any
footage having been released so far.
The policy problem, which is outlined in appendix four of the
regulatory impact statement,
is that privacy and commercially sensitive information may be
disclosed through an OIA release.
However,
the OIA
has always provided for the withholding of information
on a number of grounds, including the protection of privacy.
It appears, Mr. Chair, that there is no convincing evidence to
explain why these existing protections are inadequate.
The transparency that access to camera footage brings with it
incentivizes operators to comply with catch management
requirements. It ensures accuracy in reporting
requirements,
and as the ombudsman has submitted,
it upholds the purposes of both the OIA and the
Fisheries Act.
Mr. Chair, I also provide a comparison
to that even heavily protected national security information
where the need for secrecy is so well justified and
understood. This remains subject to
oversight and public assurance through the Inspector General of
Intelligence and Security.
The PCE recommends that the OIA should continue to
apply to onboard footage.
But if the committee is seeking assurances relating to disclosure,
we note that the ombudsman has suggested an amendment to the
OIA to that effect and is prepared to issue guidance
to assist.
The PCE supports the submission of the ombudsman, which the
committee should carefully consider.
Finally, Mr. Chair, turning to the interface between the Fisheries Act
and RMA reform.
Through the decision of the Court of Appeal in the case of Mōtiti,
it was confirmed that the RMA had a wider focus than the Fisheries
Act, protecting biodiversity and natural character
values in the coastal marine area for their own sake
rather than for their connection to the utilization of fisheries resource.
This decision provided councils with clear criteria to
apply when they are developing rules which would impact fisheries
management.
However, this was responded to by the Resource Management
Consenting and Other System Changes Amendment Act 2025,
which constrained the scope of the rules that can be imposed by
regional councils.
In doing so, it muddied the clarity that was provided by the Court of
Appeal.
It introduced a process which confuses the performance
of functions under the two acts.
For example,
despite being prohibited by RMA Section 30
subsection 2 from undertaking functions
regarding fisheries management,
the process requires council to comprehensively assess
the impacts on fishing, despite regional councils not
being fisheries experts.
The concurrence of the Director General of MPI on proposed
rules is also required,
though their decision to concur is limited in its
scope and does not include consideration of the
purpose or the principles of the Fisheries Act.
These changes to the RMA are carried over into the Planning Bill
and Natural Environment Bill, which are currently before another
select committee.
This bill seeks to weaken environmental
considerations that fisheries decisions should be
having regard to.
The Fisheries Act, if amended, would no longer provide
assurance that fisheries management will be grounded in the
biophysical constraints of the marine environment.
Weakening environmental considerations under both
legislative schemes that are supposed to protect marine
biodiversity
means considerable riskFor degradation of the marine
environment and fisheries.
Mr. Chair, that concludes the prepared comments from the PCE, and we're happy to
take questions from members.
Very good.
Thank you.
We've got a question from Rachel first.
Okay. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thank you for your submission.
I'm keen to ask for your thoughts on part of the bill that you haven't
actually commented on. So I'm hoping you have considered this,
and that's the part around the carry forward of ACE.
So, as you'll be aware, there's an annual catch
entitlement, and
there are some species that can currently have up to 10%
carry forward. And what the bill would allow is, in extreme
circumstances, a 100% carry forward if the
minister made that determination.
There have been some concerns raised about that, which I share, around
that could have an impact on the sustainability of a stock.
So I'm just interested in whether you've considered that particular part of the
bill,
any suggestions you might have to improve it,
any concerns you might have around the sustainability measures.
We haven't looked at that one, unfortunately.
No, not in any great detail.
Okay.
Yeah.
Okay.
We're more than happy to receive questions, Mr.
Chair, from members if you would like written feedback from the PCE on this.
I'd really appreciate that one. So the particular issue, there's some really good
examples
where it has been used and then hasn't been used.
So rock lobster gives some good examples.
During COVID, there was an order in council so that they could
transfer quota after a market collapse, and then they
considered it
after Cyclone Gabrielle and decided not to do a
carryover. The concern that's been raised is at the moment it's
10%, this would allow 100%, and is there
enough information on the stock, all of those things.
So yeah, if you could provide us with some written advice on that particular issue,
I'd appreciate that.
Yeah. I think what we could do afterwards is
get the-
Some written questions
... written questions.
Yeah.
Yeah. Steve, you had a question.
Yeah.
Thank you very much for the submission.
In your decision making
under uncertainty, you point out the effective assessment requires
biological and ecological information, catch composition, size, age structure,
to estimate biomass, population dynamics, all of these factors.
And yet, if I understood correctly, you just articulated that you did
support the minister taking into consideration environmental,
social, and cultural factors as well as the biological.
My concern in that is what are, particularly the
economic, social, or cultural factors.
Culture is in a different category, I think, but what are the economic or
social factors that would override a
biological need to reduce the
catch?
Yeah. As
I indicated, we see that as a short term consideration, and I
think
the current drafting of the bill does allow for
those considerations to be taken
into the decision making process when thinking about
a transition. So if
reductions have to be made, how is that
managed from an economic and a social perspective?
And I think the current system
is working well in that respect.
So I think that in the short term, those sorts of factors can be
taken into consideration, but in the long term, it's inappropriate.
As a supplement.
Yeah.
It seems to me a concern, and it seems to me a
weakening of the basic
evaluation of the ecological health of the stock
to
allow the minister to consider economic
things, unless that's within a very narrow
scope of something like what you just described.
So would you want to see greater clarification
of the extent to which those factors could be considered,
and in such a way as they don't override the
ecological and biological reality of the health of the ecosystem and the stock?
Mm-hmm.
Yeah.
Yes, and I think that the current drafting of the act allows for that.
Oh.
Yeah.
Right. Rachel.
Just to sup on that, because this has been a topic of discussion
amongst submitters around the factors that we've, I call them.
I guess
this is something you might need to come back to us again as a written question.
Would you consider, I guess, a hierarchy of the factors in
terms of the importance of which ones are considered?
Because I think that's one of the things.
What's the hierarchy and the order that you would
consider them in terms of which ones carry more weight and which ones don't?
That is certainly a drafting solution that the committee could pursue-
Mm
... and request further
advice from officials about what that might look like if that was what you were
minded to do. We're having very similar conversations at the moment to the
environment committee in relation to the planning and natural environment bills in
terms of the environment, and in this case, the marine environment
will tell you what the biophysical constraints are.
It doesn't matter what decisions are made.
It doesn't matter what hierarchy of considerations you try to apply through the
law. The environment will tell you what is or is not possible.
There will or will not be fish
to be fished. So on that basis,
the submission of the commissioner has been that your biophysical
constraints must be your primary
consideration, not because this house says that
that's what is required. That is simply the environment in which you are
operating, and that will tell you what is required.
Rachel.
I do have a sup on that.
Sorry.
Which I guess one of the challenges in the ocean, and
againHappy for you to come back. The challenges in the ocean
is that we can't see all of it, right?
And so unlike making land-based assessments, in terms
of the biological impacts of the ocean,
the ability to see everything that's out there is so limited, both in terms of fish
stock, but then other parts of ocean health.
So, have you got any thoughts
about investment that's needed to
actually get that level of information so that we can make the decision about the
impact on the environment?
So these are questions that we would welcome certainly following
this submission today. Unfortunately, our chief scientist
is also currently on leave, but both he and the commissioner are returning next
week and would be the best placed expert to assist with providing
information to the committee on those matters.
But I do draw your attention to our written
submission in the section Decision Making Under Uncertainty,
where we do discuss these sorts of issues and talk about
how
a more precautionary approach might work in
that
stocks that have good information have
more flexibility than stocks with poorer information.
And those are the sorts of incentives that could be built into the system.
Yeah.
Steve, you're this up.
Yeah. I guess the concern is when you say the biophysical limits of the ocean
will be self-evident, there will or there will not be fish.
I guess what we're trying to do is manage so that there are fish because clearly
when there has been evidence that a fishery is in trouble,
there hasn't been sufficient rapidity of intervention to
protect the remaining stock so that it can recover more
quickly. The Hawke fishery being a recent example, I
guess.
And our concern, just to belabor this point
somewhat, is that if we give scope for other
considerations than simply the available evidence on the
biological and the ecological health of the fishery,
then that will tend to want to override
the evidence that the fishery can't handle the
current take.
Again, as Geoff responded, we'd point you to the relevant part of our written
submission, but we'd be certainly-
If you don't disagree with that-
We'd be happy to provide more information if you had a further question or
statement you wanted to put to the commissioner, Geoff.
Yeah. What I would say at a high level is that
the design of the QMS was intended to create
incentives for self-regulation.
And I think the fact is that that's worked in some fisheries and
not others, and that requires a much deeper
analysis on the interplay between
the different players in the fisheries system.
I think
the answer to your question would be it depends on the fishery.
Yeah.
And a lot more analysis would be required to
really get into that.
Yeah. Susan.
You said it that towards the end of your submission, it seems perverse to
knowingly undermine access to information about our fish stocks.
I just wondered what you meant by that because I would've thought that actually
we've got better information about our fish stocks now than we had sort of 20 or 30
years ago or even before and it seems to be improving.
But just in particular what you meant by it seems perverse to knowingly
undermine access to information about our fish stocks.
We draw your attention to the
paragraph under Decision Making Under Uncertainty, which
catalogs that there has been, in recent years, a fall in the
overall funding for research both from
the fisheries sector and the Crown.
So that's what we are referring to there.
Okay.
So talking about the... Sorry, you're talking about the amount of research,
the funding of research-
That's right
... specifically?
Yeah.
Okay.
I'll give my burning question to my
colleague-
So just-
... Susan.
Yeah. Just-
Last question
burning question is you talked about fish stocks as being the kind of
primary
consideration, but would you agree that actually ocean health as well
matters and that there needs to, that it's not just about fish
stock, but actually the importance of the role the ocean
plays,
yeah, is an important consideration.
Again, I draw your attention to our written submission, in
the last section on failure to move towards an integrated approach to fisheries
management.
Some of the criticisms
that exist about the QMS currently are that it takes
a single species approach.
And what the commissioner is saying here is that really these
changes are doubling down on that.
We're moving even more to a
more single species approach
than
previously.
So
yes, of course, we would advocate for a system which
takes a broader perspective, but as I said
previously,
the tools that exist within the system are not ideal
for that. And we have spoken
previously in other submissions about the need for marine
spatial planning.
Yeah.
And that that sort of approach would give you more tools to
deal with ecosystem impacts,
rather than trying to do everything through setting tac.
Yeah.
Thank you very much. We've come to time.
And I'm sure we'll have you back in
at some stage in the future for various issues, so.
Great.
And yeah, do
get in touch with written questions-
Yeah
... we'll get to.
We'll probably put together some question
far through for post-hearing
inquiry. Thank you. Got them. Thank you very much.
Right. Next we've got a
change to the
agenda.
We were
due to have
Gisborne Fisheries- Yeah, what happened to Selvadurai? Selvadurai. He's on a plane.
Yeah. So- Delayed. I don't know. He said
he couldn't make it. He's on a plane ...
we've got Deep Sea Conservation Coalition
instead.
So they're going to be by Zoom or-
Online ... they're online now.
Good morning. Welcome to the Primary Production Select Committee.
We have Duncan Currie, who's the legal advisor for
Deep Sea Conservation Coalition online.
You have 10 minutes. How you use that time's up to you.
So, fire away.
Thank you, Chair, and good morning members of the committee.
Tena koutou.
Thank you for the opportunity to speak today.
Our interest in this bill is simple.
We are seeking stronger protection for the ocean, particularly from
destructive fishing methods such as bottom trawling on seamounts and similar deep
sea features.
This bill takes New Zealand's fisheries management in the opposite direction.
Our recommendation is that the Fisheries Amendment Bill be withdrawn
in its entirety.
We do not say this because fisheries management is perfect, it is not.
Reform is needed, but this bill is not the reform that New Zealand needs.
It weakens environmental safeguards, reduces transparency,
shuts non-industry voices out of decision-making, and makes it harder for
unlawful decisions to be challenged.
I will focus on five core concerns.
Firstly, that the bill removes fundamental environmental guardrails from fisheries
management.
The Fisheries Act currently contains clear environmental principles.
Those principles require decision-makers to maintain associated and
dependent species above a level that ensures their long-term
viability, that's Section 9A,
maintain biological diversity in Section 9B, and protect
habitats of particular significance for fisheries management in Section 9C.
These are the foundation of sustainable fisheries management.
They're not optional extras.
But the proposed Clause 13F would allow the minister, in setting
catch limits, to take into account
adverse environmental effects and environmental principles only to the extent they
are relevant to narrower standard factors.
In practical terms, this could mean the minister could treat a fishery in isolation
rather than part of a wide ocean ecosystem.
It risks taking us backwards towards a narrow single species
approach. This is inconsistent with science,
it is bad policy, and it is inconsistent with New Zealand's international
obligations.
Under the Law of the Sea Convention, New Zealand must take into account the effects
of fishing on species associated with or dependent
upon harvested species.
And under the UN Fish Stocks Agreement, New Zealand must assess impacts
on target stocks, associated independent species, and the
wider ecosystem and apply the precautionary approach.
Clause 13F risks
New Zealand routinely
failing against these obligations.
Secondly, the bill shuts stakeholders and the public out of
decision-making.
The bill would allow the minister to set TACs for multiple years, with quotas
rolling over automatically.
Once that multi-year decision is made, there may be no annual opportunity
for stakeholders to scrutinize the settings, respond to new information, or
raise concerns.
This is deeply concerning. Fish populations can decline,
ecosystems can change, climate impacts can intensify,
new bycatch information can emerge, and new habitat impacts can be
withdrawn. Yet under this bill, decisions could effectively continue on
autopilot.
This bill also allows non-regulatory measures to proceed without consultation
for any reason.
This is an extraordinarily broad discretion and could apply,
for example, to voluntary measures proposed by industry.
This would shut out public tangata whenua, environmental organizations,
rec fishers, and other stakeholders out of decisions affecting the
health of the ocean.
Third, the bill erodes transparency by burying onboard camera
footage. You've heard a great deal about this.
Cameras help build trust, but only if the information they
gather can be meaningfully scrutinized.
In effect, footage of what happens on fishing vessels may never see
the light of day.
Privacy concerns can be addressed through proportionate tools such as blurring
faces, releasing still images, releasing short
segments, redacting sensitive material, or providing
summaries where necessary.
The maximum fine for releasing camera footage also creates an
absurd imbalance. You've heard a great deal about this.
Fourth, the bill risks incentivizing less
selective and more destructive fishing.
We're particularly concerned about the changes to deemed
values and discarding rules. Deemed values are supposed to create a
financial disincentive for catching fish above quota
or catching species a fisher does not have quota for.
But the bill would allow a lower alternative deemed value for
non-targeted fish immediately frozen at sea.
The current minister has already indicated an intention to use this for
deep water freezer vessels.This would create a
perverse incentive for industrial offshore fishing methods,
including bottom trawling, over more selective and low-impact
methods.
Fifth, the bill sets an unworkable timeframe for judicial review.
The proposed Clause 313A would restrict the
timeframe
for filing judicial review proceedings to 20 working days.
This is simply unrealistic and insulates decision-making from lawful
scrutiny.
So what should be done? We agree that fisheries reform is needed, but
it should be a reform that starts from the need for a healthy ocean, because
healthy fisheries depend on healthy ecosystems.
More than 100,000 people have supported calls to end bottom
trawling on seamounts and similar deep sea features, and
more than 73,000 people recently supported Live Ocean's call
to action to phase out bottom trawling, including the immediate protection of
seamounts.
This is a public mandate for stronger ocean protection.
This is not a public mandate for weakening environmental principles,
reducing transparency, limiting consultation, and restricting access
to judicial review.
In closing, we recommend that the committee recommends this entire bill be
withdrawn,
and instead the government begins a genuine review of fisheries management,
one which does not take this bill as its starting point, and one that asks how
New Zealand can move towards selective, sustainable,
low-impact fishing while protecting taonga species, critical
features like seamounts and other vulnerable marine ecosystems.
The review should consider the work already being done through the EEZ
Benthic Forum
and the recommendations made by environmental NGOs to protect seamounts.
This bill weakens the environmental principles of the Fisheries Act, gives
too much discretion and power to ministers,
reduces transparency and public participation, and hides information
from scrutiny. It makes it harder to
challenge unlawful decisions.
In closing, New Zealand can have fisheries that provides food and
livelihoods, and you've heard this today.
A prosperity of fisheries begins with the protection of the ocean they depend upon.
This bill will not achieve that. For these reasons and others,
in our written submission, the Deep Sea Conservation Coalition urges the
committee to recommend that the Fisheries Amendment Bill be
withdrawn in its entirety. Ngā mihi.
Thank you very much, Duncan. Now we're going to a question from
Steve Abel.
Yeah. Thank you, Duncan, for such a substantive and clear
submission. Can you just expand,
for the benefit of the committee, on the Clause 40 deemed value stuff
and how that
could incentivize more bottom trawling over
more sustainable fishing methods
and the perverse incentive it creates?
Yeah, that's right. Well, it's really getting it the other way around because
deemed values are supposed to, as I said, create a financial disincentive for
catching fish above quota or catching
species
that a fisher does not have quota for.
Yeah.
But the bill would allow a lower alternative deemed value to be
set for non-targeted fish immediately frozen at sea.
And that's where the perverse incentive would be.
It would move the incentive towards offshore fishing

I'd just like to draw the committee to a few key
points that we find particularly troubling.
Firstly, the ability to consider social,
cultural, and economic factors when determining rebuild periods
needs to be removed.
The proposed amendments in Clause 10 of the bill seek to overturn the
Supreme Court decision on tarakihi, and
we say they are inconsistent with the statutory purpose of the act.
Under Section 13 ( 2 ) of the Fisheries Act, the minister
is required to set a TAC for a stock at a level that will
enable the stock's recovery within a period
appropriate to the stock and any environmental
conditions affecting that stock.
Under Section 13 ( 3 ) , the minister is to have regard to relevant
social, cultural, and economic factors when considering the
way in which and rate at which a stock is moved
towards or above a level that can produce the maximum
sustainable yield.
Now, the Supreme Court in the tarakihi decision confirmed that the
minister must assess the period appropriate to the stock by
reference to the biological
characteristics and environmental conditions, which I understand
other submitters have referred to as the biophysical limits,
and without regard to social, cultural, and
economic factors.The court also found that where
the stock's biological characteristics and environmental conditions
allow for more than one appropriate recovery period,
that's where the social, cultural, and economic factors can then
be engaged. That's where that can
be referred to in selecting an alternative or the
appropriate rebuild period.
Now, the bill replaces this approach with the requirement to only
consider the scientifically appropriate period as one of
many competing factors, including social and economic
factors that the minister considers relevant.
If rebuild periods are set by reference to social and
economic factors, the fishing industry is then
able to influence the rebuild period and make it entirely
lawful to set longer and more unsustainable
rebuild periods, and longer rebuild periods allow
higher total allowable catches.
The outcome is a fish stock that is held at a very
low level for longer, making the stock more vulnerable to sharp
decline if unpredictable environmental changes are
experienced, and this reduces the species' ability to have
a role in the wider ecosystem.
For these reasons, scientific and environmental
considerations should override other factors when determining the
rebuild period.
Such considerations are fundamentally about the health and sustainability
of the fishery, the wider ecosystem, and basically
ensuring future generations can also fish.
If the bill is not thrown out, we seek that sections
13B, clauses four, five, and six, and we can
provide these to the committee afterwards, are removed.
And we also seek that sections 13 ( 2 ) and
13 ( 3 ) of the act are left alone.
Ultimately, you can't enjoy the social, cultural,
and economic benefits of a thriving fishery without the fish in the
first place.
The next point is about the minimum size
limits in the fisheries regulations.
We understand the government was going to drop this proposed

The bill
proposes to amend the fisheries commercial fishing regulations to
remove minimum size limits.

cycles, reduces future breeding capacity, and risks the total
collapse of fish stocks.
Length limits should be retained to discourage their take.
The ability to carry over ACE under
proposed new section 67C and 67D
should also be removed.
Saving uncaught annual catch entitlement and carrying it

potentially causing a catastrophic decline in fish stocks, which are

If a higher than expected catch happens due to carry forward,

predator-prey relationships.
